Q1: I organized my study routine around listening and audio resources since that's how I learn best. I'd dedicate about 24 hours a week to studying, but honestly, a lot of that happened outside the LMS because I don't have reliable internet access at home. I would download lecture recordings when I could get to campus or a library, then listen to them repeatedly on my phone. My schedule was pretty structured because I knew I had to make the most of every opportunity to access materials.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on when I could actually get online, which wasn't always predictable. I tried to maintain some regularity, like going to the library on certain days, but deadlines would throw everything off. When assignments were due, I'd get really anxious and spend entire days at places with wifi just to make sure I could submit everything. The inconsistency stressed me out a lot, even though my grades ended up being good.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I absolutely needed to—checking for new assignments, submitting work, or downloading materials to study offline later. Sometimes the fear of missing something important would push me to log in even when I wasn't sure there was anything new. My anxiety about falling behind was a constant motivator, honestly. I'd also check discussion boards occasionally to see if classmates had asked questions I needed answers to.
Q4: It never really became a smooth routine because of my internet situation. What became routine was my trips to places with wifi—that was the pattern, not the LMS use itself. I'd batch everything together: download videos, check announcements, post to discussions, all in one session. It felt more like a survival strategy than a natural habit.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I'm an auditory learner. I'd download them and listen multiple times, sometimes while doing other things. The discussion boards were also helpful because I could see how other students understood concepts, which helped me check my own thinking. Anything I could take offline and engage with repeatedly felt most valuable.
Q6: I often ignored the interactive elements that required constant internet connection, like live chat features or real-time collaborative tools. I also delayed watching videos directly on the platform because streaming was unreliable for me. Some supplementary reading materials I skipped because I couldn't easily listen to them—I really needed audio formats. It wasn't that these resources weren't good; they just didn't fit my situation.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could tell it would directly help me understand the material or prepare for exams. I also valued anything I could engage with on my own schedule, offline. If something felt like busywork or required me to be online at specific times, my anxiety would spike and I'd question whether it was worth the stress. The payoff had to be clear.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford given my limited online access. Required activities got my full attention because I was scared of the consequences of missing them. With optional ones, I'd weigh whether the trip to get wifi was worth it for something that wouldn't affect my grade. Sometimes I felt guilty about skipping them, but I had to prioritize.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings, PDFs, and any materials I could save, then study from those for days without touching the LMS. I also kept detailed handwritten notes that I'd record myself reading aloud so I could listen to them later. My textbooks and personal audio recordings were probably more important to my learning than anything the system tracked.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was intensely studying—listening to lectures on repeat, going through my notes, practicing problems—but the LMS would show almost nothing. My engagement looked low because I wasn't logging in, but I was actually deeply immersed in the material. The disconnect between my actual effort and what appeared online was huge and honestly kind of frustrating.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my entire approach. I'd access something once, download it, and then that material would be my companion for days or weeks. A single video might get watched ten times on my phone, but the system only saw one view. I became really efficient at extracting everything I needed in brief online sessions so I could learn properly offline.
Q12: My anxiety would sometimes get so overwhelming that I'd freeze up, even when I knew I needed to work. There were periods when the fear of not being able to access materials or submit on time made it hard to even start. Also, when multiple assignments piled up and I couldn't get online as often as needed, I felt completely overwhelmed. The mindfulness exercises I tried helped a bit, but the scared feeling never fully went away.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or had competing responsibilities, I'd have to make hard choices about what to prioritize. Fatigue made everything feel more frightening and urgent. Sometimes I'd log into the LMS in a panic, not really processing what I was seeing, just trying to make sure I wasn't missing something critical. My usage patterns were heavily influenced by my stress levels.
Q14: There were times I'd scroll through the LMS feeling completely disconnected, like I was going through the motions. My anxiety would have me checking and rechecking things without actually absorbing information. I'd open discussion posts or announcements but my mind would be racing about other worries. Those sessions felt empty even though they technically counted as 'activity.'
Q15: I postponed things when my anxiety got too intense—it's like the fear of doing something imperfectly paralyzed me. Even knowing an assignment was important, I'd sometimes avoid it because starting felt too scary. I'd tell myself I'd do it when I could get to better wifi, but really I was just putting off the stress. Eventually the deadline pressure would force me to act.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my motivator. They created intense anxiety spikes that made me feel sick sometimes, but they also forced me to overcome my avoidance. I'd plan my wifi trips around due dates, making sure I had access when I needed to submit. The system probably showed bursts of activity right before deadlines, which accurately reflected my panic-driven work patterns.
Q17: Good feedback motivated me to keep pushing despite my fears. When I saw my exam scores or assignment grades were high, it reassured me that my offline study methods were working. Negative or unclear feedback increased my anxiety significantly and sometimes made me hesitant to engage with similar activities. I really needed that positive reinforcement to keep going.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear deadlines gave me targets to work toward, which I needed. But the expectation of regular online participation was hard given my access limitations. I appreciated that assignments could be submitted anytime before the deadline rather than requiring real-time presence. The flexibility in when I could engage saved me.
Q19: Downloadable materials made engagement much easier—anything I could take offline was a gift. The video lectures being available for download was crucial. What made it harder was any feature requiring consistent internet access or synchronous participation. Discussion post requirements were stressful because I couldn't always respond in a timely way. The design assumed internet access I didn't have.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something, trying to understand the material but struggling to produce work that meets their standards. They could also be anxious like me, checking everything repeatedly without making progress. Or maybe they're engaged with the platform socially but struggling academically. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. My LMS activity looked minimal, but I was working hard offline and my grades showed it. I think students who know how to learn independently can succeed without constant platform engagement. The system measures presence, not learning. Someone like me, who downloads and studies offline, will always look less engaged than they actually are.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone accessed materials and submitted work—the mechanical parts of being a student. But they miss the depth of engagement, the hours spent with downloaded materials, the anxiety behind every login, the real learning happening away from the screen. They capture shadows of engagement, not the substance. My logs would tell a story of a barely-there student, which couldn't be further from the truth.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, thinking about it even when you're not 'studying,' and caring about understanding it deeply. For me, it's those moments when I'm listening to a lecture for the fifth time and something finally clicks. It's not about how often I log in—it's about whether the course lives in my mind. True engagement is internal and often invisible.
Q24: The LMS can't show my fear or my determination to succeed despite it. It can't capture the hours I spent listening to recordings on my phone while walking to save bus fare, or the anxiety attacks before deadlines, or the relief when I finally understood a concept. It doesn't show that I'm a scared person who shows up anyway, who figured out how to learn without reliable internet. My resilience is invisible to the data.
